text: This is the case of right upper lobe non-small cell lung carcinoma with colloid goiter of right thyroid lobe.
Plan is of right upper lobectomy with right hemothyroidectomy.
The approach that was used was partial split sternotomy with anterior thoracotomy at fourth intercostal space.
Initially, posterior hilum dissection was performed.

as we can see a structure being shown is esophagus along which runs the right vagus nerve as shown by the dbq forceps structures that can be identified are left inferior pulmonary vein left main bronchus right main bronchus station 7 carina lymph nodes have already been removed here
So right upper lobe bronchus and bronchus intermedius are identified.
In the lateral aspect and towards the horizontal fissure structures that can be identified are the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius.
The ascending A2 artery lies just in front of the right upper lobe bronchus which arises from the interlobar artery.
Central vein lies behind
the ascending A2 artery in this case and a branch of it that is the V2T can be visualized.
The ongoing interlobar artery divides into lower lobe artery and the middle lobe artery.
Lower lobe artery further divides into segmental branches that is the A6, A9 plus 10 and A7 plus 8.
Moving on to the anterior hilum, the structures that can be identified are the azygous vein that drains into the superior vena cava.
Below it lies the truncus superior, which is the first branch of the right pulmonary artery.
Then it further continues as the interlobar artery after giving off the truncus superior branch.
Truncus Superior typically gives two branches as seen here, A1 and A3 branches.
The AT branch has further two branches, A3A and A3B.
Similarly, the A1 branch of the Truncus Superior gives off three branches.
First one is the A1A, A1B,
and the recurrent A2 branch, which goes towards the posterior segment.
When performing the right upper lobectomy, the truncus superior and its branches as well as the ascending A2 branches are divided.
The interior most structure seen in the anterior hilum is the superior pulmonary vein.
Small branching from the superior pulmonary vein towards the middle lobe can be seen, which is the V4-V5.
Generally, the right upper lobe vein can be divided into V1, central vein, and the V4-V5 branch.
V1 vein can divide into two subsegments, V1a and V1b.
The V1B vein makes the boundary between the S1 and S3 segment.
That is above the V1 vein is the S1 or apical segment and below the V1B vein is the S3 or anterior segment.
V3B vein can also be seen arising from the V1 vein here.
Central vein that can be identified here branches into V2A, V2B, V2C, V2T and V3B.
V1 vein and the central vein are divided while performing a right upper lobectomy.
